channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Review: A New ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Metropolitan Opera
item.description: Yuval Sharon’s debut production featured an astonishing performance by the soprano Lise Davidsen. It is the event of the season.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:description: Michael Spyres, left, and Lise Davidsen in the tunnel as the title lovers in “Tristan und Isolde” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: Harry Styles and 8 More Boy Band Stars Who Went Solo
item.description: As the former One Direction singer releases his fourth album, listen back to how Bobby Brown, Ricky Martin and others made their own statements.
item.media:description: Harry Styles onstage at the Brit Awards earlier this month.

item.title: Dozens of States Weigh Continuing Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: After the Justice Department reached a settlement with the concert giant, the judge overseeing the trial asked the states and the company to do the same.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: The Justice Department, joined by 39 states and the District of Columbia, sued Live Nation in May 2024, arguing that the company was a monopoly that dominated the music industry.

item.title: To Pay the Bills, a Cult Band Becomes a Doors Tribute Act
item.description: With even critically acclaimed bands struggling to make a living, the British group Field Music has come up with a novel way to top up its income.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Joanna Yee
item.media:credit: Joanna Yee for The New York Times

item.title: Suspect in Rihanna Shooting Is Charged With Attempted Murder
item.description: A woman fired a semiautomatic weapon at Rihanna’s home while she was there with ASAP Rocky and their three children on Sunday, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: Nobody was injured in the shooting at Rihanna’s Los Angeles mansion on Sunday.

item.title: How ‘Sync Music’ Became the Soundtrack to Our Lives
item.description: “Sync music” has become the soundtrack to our lives — whether we realize it or not.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Francis Bradley
item.media:credit: Emily Shur for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kara Talve (left) and Anze Rozman, who are married to each other, collaborating on a composition at Bleeding Fingers studios in Santa Monica, Calif.

item.title: New York Philharmonic Announces 2026-27 Season
item.description: A new era for the orchestra begins in September, when Gustavo Dudamel officially becomes its music and artistic director.
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, who will officially become the New York Philharmonic’s music and artistic director in the 2026-27 season.

item.title: At 100, György Kurtág Is Still Searching for the Right Note
item.description: György Kurtág, the eminent composer and Hungarian national treasure, has reached a rare milestone yet shows little interest in retirement.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer György Kurtág in 2018. He recently celebrated his 100th birthday in Budapest.

item.title: After Kennedy Center Exit, Washington National Opera Returns
item.description: Washington National Opera managed to resume performances within two months of its abrupt departure. But there are still challenges ahead.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Washington National Opera’s new production of Scott Joplin’s “Treemonisha,” which opened at Lisner Auditorium at George Washington University on Saturday.

item.title: Review: Harry Styles Hits the Club on ‘Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.’
item.description: The pop star’s new album revels in the communal experience of clubbing. But his gleaming songs don’t reveal much about the man behind them.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:description: “Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.” is the fourth solo album by the former One Direction member Harry Styles.

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster’s Owner, Settles Antitrust Case With Justice Dept.
item.description: The agreement, which requires a judicial sign-off, would avoid a breakup of the concert giant. But states that joined the suit object to the terms.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario, David McCabe and Olivia Bensimon
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The tentative settlement ends a case that had bipartisan political support in Washington.

item.title: After a Shooting at Rihanna’s Home, a Woman Is Held on Suspicion of Attempted Murder
item.description: No injuries were reported after the shooting on Sunday. A 35-year-old woman is being held on roughly $10 million bail, jail records show.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Mike Coppola/Getty Images For The Gotham Film
item.media:description: Rihanna has three children with her partner, ASAP Rocky.

item.title: Country Joe McDonald, Whose Antiwar Song Became an Anthem, Dies at 84
item.description: One of the starring acts at Woodstock, he and his band, the Fish, came out of the Bay Area’s psychedelic rock scene. He went on to a long career as a solo artist.
item.dc:creator: Jim Farber
item.category: Country Joe and the Fish (Music Group)
item.media:description: Country Joe McDonald in 1981. The tone of the politics and social commentary in his songs could range from whimsical to snarky.

item.title: Conductor Andris Nelsons Has Become a Cautionary Tale
item.description: Andris Nelsons’s abrupt departure from the Boston Symphony Orchestra shouldn’t be surprising to those who have witnessed his artistic decline.
item.dc:creator: David Allen

item.title: Sturgill Simpson, Olivia Rodrigo: 8 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Sturgill Simpson’s political screed, Olivia Rodrigo’s Magnetic Fields cover and the Lunar New Year song burning up the charts in Vietnam.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Sturgill Simpson released his new album as Johnny Blue Skies & the Dark Clouds on YouTube.

item.title: Boston Symphony Abruptly Ends Andris Nelsons’ Contract
item.description: The orchestra’s leadership announced on Friday that it and the conductor Andris Nelsons “were not aligned on future vision.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney

item.title: Bob Power on His Work on A Tribe Called Quest’s ‘The Low End Theory’
item.description: A Tribe Called Quest had the vision for “The Low End Theory.” The engineer Bob Power helped piece it together.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/WireImage for The Recording Academy, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bob Power’s work on A Tribe Called Quest’s “The Low End Theory” has been lauded by listeners and critics since its release 35 years ago.

item.title: Top National Symphony Leader Quits in New Blow to Kennedy Center
item.description: The executive director, Jean Davidson, said her departure reflects frustration at the turmoil that has engulfed the arts center.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s no secret that this has been a really hard year,” the executive director of the National Symphony Orchestra, Jean Davidson, said of her decision to leave her post after less than three years.

item.title: A Philharmonic Conductor’s Concerts Surprise, for Better and Worse
item.description: Mirga Grazinyte-Tyla returned to the New York Philharmonic for a pair of programs, but only one formed a cohesive arc of ideas.
item.dc:creator: Corinna da Fonseca-Wollheim

item.title: When Britney Spears’s Conservatorship Ended, Concern for Her Did Not
item.description: The pop star’s arrest on suspicion of driving under the influence this week was a breaking point, years after she regained control of her life and finances.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:description: Some observers have questioned whether Britney Spears, after successfully fighting to be released from a 13-year conservatorship, now needs more supervision.

item.title: From 2001: Aaliyah, 22, Singer Who First Hit the Charts at 14
item.description: She was seen as a hip-hop temptress when she was still a teenager, and her albums “Age Ain’t Nothing but a Number” and “One in a Million” sold millions of copies.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:description: Aaliyah in 1995.

item.title: From 1983: Karen Carpenter, 32, Is Dead; Singer Teamed With Brother
item.description: The Carpenters sold more than 30 million records with the irresistible combination of her soft-rock contralto and her brother’s lush arrangements.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Karen Carpenter performing around 1975.

item.title: From 1995: Grammy-Winning Singer Selena Killed in Shooting at Texas Motel
item.description: Known as the queen of Tejano music, she was beloved as an idol and a heartthrob on both sides of the Mexican border.
item.dc:creator: Sam Howe Verhovek
item.media:credit: Dave Einsel/Houston Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Selena performing in Houston in 1993.

item.title: From 1977: Maria Callas, 53, Is Dead of Heart Attack in Paris
item.description: Considered the most exciting opera singer of her time, she thrilled audiences with her penchant for spectacle onstage and in her personal life.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Ericson
item.media:description: Maria Callas in the 2017 documentary “Maria By Callas,” directed by Tom Volf.

item.title: From 1959: Billie Holiday Dies Here at 44; Jazz Singer Had Wide Influence
item.description: Miss Holiday, who became a singer more out of desperation than desire, was one of the most influential jazz musicians of her time.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Billie Holiday performing in Manhattan in 1947.

item.title: From 2011: Amy Winehouse, British Soul Singer With a Troubled Life, Dies at 27
item.description: A British singer who found worldwide fame with her sassy, hip-hop-inflected take on retro soul, she became a tabloid fixture because of addiction problems.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Amy Winehouse sold millions of albums and won five Grammy Awards.

item.title: From 2018: Aretha Franklin, Indomitable ‘Queen of Soul,’ Dies at 76
item.description: With hits like “Respect” and “Chain of Fools,” she defined a female archetype: sensual and strong, long-suffering but ultimately indomitable.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Don Hogan Charles/The New York Times
item.media:description: Aretha Franklin in 1968, performing at the Democratic National Convention in Chicago.

item.title: Inside Harry Styles’s ‘Kiss All the Time. Disco, Occasionally.’ With Kid Harpoon
item.description: The producer and songwriter has been responsible for some of this century’s biggest hits. His greatest skill may be remaining curious.
item.dc:creator: Grayson Haver Currin
item.category: Kiss All the Time, Disco Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: Adali Schell for The New York Times
item.media:description: Tom Hull, a.k.a. Kid Harpoon, at home in California. He dragged a bunch of modular synths to recording sessions with Harry Styles for the pop star’s new album.

item.title: Three 6 Mafia on Their Oscars Win, 20 Years Later
item.description: Twenty years ago, Three 6 Mafia’s Oscars triumph for “It’s Hard Out Here for a Pimp” helped an uptight awards show loosen up.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: The members of Three 6 Mafia celebrating their win onstage at the Oscars in 2006.

item.title: Britney Spears Is Arrested on Suspicion of D.U.I. in California
item.description: “Hopefully this can be the first step in long overdue change that needs to occur in Britney’s life,” a representative for the pop star said after she was released from jail.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Britney Spears was pulled over after officers observed her driving erratically at high speeds, a California Highway Patrol spokesman said.

item.title: Druski Talks About His Influences, Hollywood Ambitions and Timothée Chalamet
item.description: The comedian is building a galaxy of collaborators — Timothée Chalamet, Kai Cenat and Justin Bieber — and telling us how they fit into his future.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Joe Coscarelli
item.media:credit: Jason Nocito for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Theory of Flames’ Dives Down the Rabbit Hole of Paranoia
item.description: Michel van der Aa’s “Theory of Flames” originates from a question: “How can we relate to people if we don’t believe in the same reality?”
item.dc:creator: Hugh Morris and Desiré Van Den Berg
item.media:credit: Desiré van den Berg for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michel van der Aa’s film opera “Theory of Flames,” premiering at the Dutch National Opera on Friday, centers on a troubled protagonist’s descent into conspiracy theories.

item.title: Chadwick Boseman’s Hip-Hop Play, in Shakespeare’s House
item.description: The actor Chadwick Boseman was a playwright, too. At Shakespeare’s Globe in London, his “Deep Azure” is drawing attention to a lost talent inspired by the Bard.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Magdalena Wosinska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Boseman studied theater at Howard University before becoming an actor.

item.title: How Jonathan Groff of ‘Just in Time’ Became Broadway’s Leading Man
item.description: By transmitting his love of live performance, the “Just in Time” actor has completed his ascendance to full musical stardom.
item.dc:creator: Ben Brantley and Mohamed Sadek
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: Harry Styles Left as a Dominant Male Pop Star. He Returns to a Crowd.
item.description: In the four years since the British singer last released an album, artists like Sombr, working in similar aesthetic modes, have climbed onto the charts.
item.dc:creator: Shaad D’Souza
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco Occasionally. (Album)

item.title: Squeeze Wrote a Rock Opera Five Decades Ago. It’s Coming Out Now.
item.description: During its early days, the band came up with songs about a fictional nightclub. After a career filled with ups and downs, it finally returned to “Trixies.”
item.dc:creator: David Peisner
item.media:credit: Jono White for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Difford and Glenn Tilbrook of Squeeze. Their new (old) album, “Trixies,” is a fascinating window into the group’s musical roots.
